data.title: CareTrust: The REIT That's Outperforming The S&P 500
data.description:
Introduction

On my investment journey, I have come across different styles of investors. From income-focused ones like myself to growth-focused and high-yield investors, everyone's strategy should be tailored to their individual needs.

For me, being income-focused, REITs (XLRE) have played an integral part in my portfolio-building process, thanks to their higher yields.

And while many investors are turned off by the sector, particularly over the past 3 years, some REITs have performed exceptionally. And this is despite the negative sentiment surrounding them.

Due to their interest rate sensitivity and lower growth, many have lost their appeal since interest rates skyrocketed starting in 2022. However, CareTrust REIT, Inc. (CTRE) has not only continued to perform, it has outperformed the S&P 500 (SP500).

In this article, I discuss CareTrust REIT's latest earnings, fundamentals, and why, despite their strong performance, this REIT could continue outperforming over the longer term.

Previous Buy Thesis

I last covered CTRE this past August in an article titled: One Of My Highest Conviction REITs Has Crushed The Market, And I'm Still Bullish.

Since then, CareTrust REIT has continued to outperform, up nearly 11% to 6.27% for the index at the time of writing. CTRE's performance has been driven by their strong growth, a result of their higher investment activity over the past two years.

Debunking the Underperformance Myth

Most of us have heard the story of REITs being underperformers. And while a higher-for-longer interest rate environment does negatively impact REITs' cost of capital, CTRE has been the exception.

As you can see, I've been highly bullish on CTRE dating back to my first article written on them in September of 2023. Since then, the stock is up nearly 86% in comparison to roughly 53% for the S&P.

So, although some REITs have underperformed amidst rising interest rates, CareTrust REIT has debunked the myth of REITs. And with most likely to see tailwinds from lower base rates over the next 12 to 24 months, CTRE looks poised for more upside.

CTRE's performance can be attributed to their strong investments over the past two years. Their performance should be commended considering the tough economic backdrop, which has resulted in slower investment activity for many.

Year-to-date, investments have exceeded the $1.5 billion in acquisitions in 2024. Their latest acquisition saw the REIT continue to expand internationally with the purchase of two care homes for $27 million. The U.K. now accounts for 15.1% of annualized base rent, second to California at 19.5%.

The acquisition was made with cash, showcasing the strength of the company's balance sheet. At the end of Q3, their net debt to EBITDA was a very low 0.4x, one of the lowest leverages I've seen amongst any REIT. Most healthcare REITs operate in the high 4x to high 5x range.

This, and their well-staggered debt, gives the company ample room to see increased investment activity. The REIT's first debt maturity isn't until 2028, with $400 million due, and the next maturity isn't until 2030. Liquidity was strong with more than $300 million in cash and $1.2 billion in revolver capacity.

And I think going into 2026, we could see acquisitions even exceed those in 2024 and 2025. If so, this will likely continue to drive price appreciation over the long term.

Although healthcare stocks typically perform well during times of uncertainty, CTRE's long-term outperformance is something I think investors highly overlook.

For comparison, I put CareTrust up against the most popular and largest S&P ETF, Vanguard's (VOO). And you can see that over the past 1, 3, 5, and even 10 years, the REIT has significantly outperformed the ETF.

1-year: 25.93% vs. 13.15%

3 years: 100.16% vs. 72.12%

5 years: 93.77% vs. 88.34%

10 years: 255.22% vs. 225.22%

10-year total return: 476.03% vs. 286.51%

Although CTRE's yield isn't as attractive as other high-quality REITs like Realty Income's (O) 5.62% or Agree Realty's (ADC) 4.08%, there's still a respectable 3.46%.

Additionally, REITs that have lower yields have seen stronger price appreciation, which improves their cost of capital. This also enables them to issue shares well above their NAVs to raise capital for future growth.

During their latest quarter in early November, CTRE's results were mixed, with a miss on FFO by $0.02. Revenue of $132.44 million beat by roughly $10 million and grew a staggering 71% from $77.4 million the year prior.

More importantly, CTRE grew every metric during the quarter, as evidenced by the chart below. And they ended Q3 with a healthy payout ratio of 74%.

FAD, or funds available for distribution, similar to AFFO, grew from $0.37 to $0.45 on a per-share basis. In total, growth was strong, up roughly 51% to $95.1 million. As far as investments, CTRE made $59 million in acquisitions and $437 million subsequent to the quarter's end. Their pipeline was also strong at $600 million.

Significant Upside May Be Limited

Using management's guidance midpoint of $1.765, this gives CTRE a forward multiple of 21.26x at the current price. Although I remain bullish on the REIT for the long term, I suggest investors wait for any signs of share price weakness, preferably around $32 or below.

At the current price, CareTrust REIT appears overvalued, as they sit well above their normal multiple of 14.27x. However, due to their strong performance and higher growth, I think it's fair to assume they'll likely trade anywhere between 18x and 20x going forward and are currently fairly valued.

Wall Street remains bullish on the stock with a price target near $40 a share. Still, this gives investors little margin of safety, as the current share price sits closer to $38 a share.

Risks

I think CTRE's biggest risk is they could see potential downside or lower returns due to their strong price appreciation. Going forward, price appreciation could be very limited, suggesting investors should wait for a pullback closer to the $30 level.

Additionally, the recent uptick in unemployment could be an indication of a slower 2026 despite the economy appearing to remain resilient.

As a result, I think the chances of a recession are higher than many are leading us to believe. If so, acquisition activity could slow dramatically in the coming years, leading to lower growth.

Bottom Line

Although I remain cautiously bullish on CareTrust REIT shares due to potential tailwinds from lower base rates, macro uncertainty remains a risk going forward.

If we do manage to see a recession, I suspect investment activity could slow, leading to lower growth and less upside.

And while I expect the REIT to navigate, their chances of beating the S&P going forward are lower since CTRE appears fairly valued here. For investors looking to buy, I think you could get a margin of safety near the $30 level.

data.title: BITW: Expensive Fund With Near Perfect Correlation To Bitcoin
data.description:
The Bitwise 10 Crypto Index Fund (OTC:BITW) is a diversified cryptocurrency investment strategy designed to provide investors with exposure to the top 10 cryptocurrencies by market capitalization. BITW can potentially be utilized by investors seeking a diversified portfolio of cryptocurrencies; however, the vast majority of assets are allocated to bitcoin, resulting in near-perfect correlation between BITW and bitcoin. Given the relative underperformance when compared to the historical performance of bitcoin and the challenging market dynamics in the cryptocurrency market, I am recommending BITW with a Sell rating.

About Bitwise 10 Crypto Index Fund

BITW was launched on November 22, 2017 as a Trust with Bitwise Investment Advisers as the Sponsor of the Trust. The Trust was later brought to the public markets on December 9, 2020 on the OTCQX Exchange. The Trust was converted to an exchange-traded product following SEC approval on November 18, 2025 and is now listed on the NYSE Arca Exchange.

BITW was designed to track the Bitwise 10 Large Cap Crypto Index, which is administered by Bitwise Index Services. The Index was designed to track the top 10 largest cryptocurrencies by market capitalization, which captures an estimated 75% of the total market capitalization of the cryptocurrency market. The Index is reconstituted monthly, and in turn, the ETF is rebalanced on a monthly basis.

BITW has a net expense ratio of 250bps, a relatively high fee when compared to peer cryptocurrency portfolio strategies. Despite offering a diversified cryptocurrency portfolio, investors must weigh whether the differentiated returns over bitcoin provide enough value to justify the relatively high management fee.

Looking through the portfolio, BITW is largely concentrated amongst the top holdings with bitcoin weighted at 74.7% and Ethereum at 14.8%. Given that portfolio weightings are dictated by market capitalization, smaller large-cap coins may be less represented in the portfolio, and thus, less influential to the value of the fund.

Looking at the performance of the portfolio, many of the smaller coins have significantly underperformed bitcoin in 2025 with bitcoin returning -6% year-to-date. In addition to this, BITW has substantially underperformed bitcoin across all historical holding periods, raising the question as to whether an investor needs to diversify across crypto assets or whether a simple investment in bitcoin will suffice.

The cryptocurrency has experienced a cascading effect in recent months, potentially driven by the imbalance between fund inflows & outflows. This is a factor that may influence the price and flows of bitcoin, particularly as more retirement accounts include an allocation to bitcoin funds. Prior to the approval of bitcoin spot ETFs in January 2024, bitcoin had a limited investor pool given the necessity for an additional brokerage account or limited options for investment vehicles. A major change that came with the emergence of bitcoin spot ETFs was the addition of advisory operations, which includes portfolio rebalances, cash raises, and charitable giving, amongst other features, adding risks that had previously not impacted bitcoin. I believe bitcoin may be exceptionally exposed given the liquidity and trading depth of the underlying asset, allowing for an appealing vehicle for capital raises.

Another driving factor in the cryptocurrency market is the use of leveraged funds. Accordingly, over $1t in market capitalization has been wiped out since October 2025 with the price of bitcoin falling by nearly -35% from its high in October 2025 before recovering some ground. Much of the price decline was driven by investors unwinding leveraged bitcoin strategies, which are estimated to be in the range of $19-30b on October 10, 2025.

Though I believe a small 1-2% allocation to cryptocurrency is appropriate for a diversified investment portfolio, investors must consider the amount of risk they are willing to take on by diversifying into smaller tokens held in BITW, such as Polkadot (DOT-USD) or Avalanche (AVAX-USD). Given the relative performance of the diversified portfolio when compared to bitcoin, investors must weight whether diversifying into riskier assets is appropriate. Overall, I believe investing in a bitcoin spot ETF like the iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT) will provide appropriate coverage in the cryptocurrency market, at least for the average investor. Given the additional risks, volatility, and relatively smaller positions of alternative coins, BITW may expose investors to more market risk than is necessary for cryptocurrency exposure.

Risks Related to BITW

BITW provides investors with a diversified portfolio strategy for investing in alternative coins, presenting certain risks that must be considered prior to making a final investment decision. Smaller cryptocurrencies may expose investors to significant volatility that may add unwanted risk to one’s portfolio. BITW is heavily concentrated in bitcoin and Ethereum and is heavily correlated with bitcoin at 97%, potentially exposing investors to greater risk and a higher management fee without a substantial amount of value being derived from the alternative coins held.

Final Thoughts

BITW provides investors with diversified exposure across the top 10 cryptocurrencies by market capitalization, differentiating exposure from bitcoin spot ETFs. Given the relative correlation of BITW to bitcoin, I do not believe that the ETF differentiates exposure enough to justify the high management fee, particularly given that the fund has underperformed bitcoin by a wide margin since inception. Given these factors, I am recommending BITW with a Sell rating.

data.title: Sprinklr: Large Customer Declines Are A Big Concern (Downgrade)
data.description:
As we come close to wrapping up 2025, it's becoming clear that the sustainability of 2025's sharp market rally is top of mind for many investors. To me, it's the right time to be rotating out of recent winners in the large-cap growth space and shifting more of our portfolios into beaten-down small- and mid-cap stocks. And yet in this space, individual stock-picking is incredibly important, and the companies I hold in my portfolio must both be cheap while also having upside fundamental catalysts to drive a re-rating.

Sprinklr (CXM), a customer experience management software company, is certainly cheap, but recent quarters have made me question its quality. The company just reported Q3 results that featured a beat on the top line but dragging subscription revenue amid an exodus of large customers. The stock is down modestly post-earnings, continuing a ~10% slide over the past year that has dramatically underperformed the S&P 500.

I last wrote a buy opinion on Sprinklr in September, when the stock was trading just shy of $8 per share. In my view, the fundamental outlook for Sprinklr has shifted downward: the company's subscription revenue growth has stalled, making us question whether the company has the chops to achieve meaningful operating leverage. Amid macro pressures that are constraining IT budgets on these "nice to have," but not mission critical software products, I'm downgrading Sprinklr back down to a neutral rating.

To me, the company is now a mixed bag of positives and negatives. On the bright side for the company:

  • Cheap multiples raise the possibility of a buyout. The number-one reason to buy Sprinklr is because it's cheap. Recently, lagging software companies have been the subject of M&A, both by strategic corporate acquirers as well as private equity (Adobe (ADBE), for example, just bought struggling SEO provider Semrush).
  • Clean balance sheet. Sprinklr has nearly ~$0.5 billion in cash on its balance sheet, unencumbered of debt: which represents a substantial chunk of its current market value. Its clean balance sheet, in my view, further raises Sprinklr's appeal as a potential M&A target.

At the same time, however, we're wary of the following factors:

  • Struggling with growth as the company pushes upmarket. Sprinklr's core strategy to grow is to chase higher-spending, larger enterprise customers. This has had mixed results. Net revenue retention rates are barely in expansion territory, growth is in the mid-single digits, and long-term RPO is declining.
  • Weak growth/profitability margin profile. Sprinklr's revenue growth plus its pro forma operating margins sit in the mid-20s, a far cry from the "Rule of 40" that most software companies aspire to, which is a justification for its steep discount versus peers.

We'll stress here that Sprinklr is quite cheap, especially after the year-to-date declines. At current share prices in the mid-$7s, Sprinklr trades at a market cap of $1.82 billion. Netting off the $480.4 million of cash on its most recent balance sheet, Sprinklr's resulting enterprise value is $1.34 billion.

One piece of good news is the fact that Sprinklr boosted its FY25 guidance. The company is now expecting 5% y/y subscription revenue growth (previously 4%) and 7% total revenue growth (previously 5%).

For next year, FY26, Wall Street is expecting Sprinklr to hit $874.5 million in revenue, or 4% y/y growth. This target appears conservative coming off a quarter in which Sprinklr grew total revenue at 9% y/y. But given the weaker net retention rates and low single-digit growth in current RPO, I find consensus targets to be quite reasonable. Taking this estimate at face value, Sprinklr takes at 1.5x EV/FY26 revenue.

Needless to say, this is an ultra-cheap multiple that reflects a lot of pessimism in Sprinklr's stock. We note that SEO specialist Semrush was in a similar boat prior to being taken out by Adobe at 3.6x revenue. Still, I also think Sprinklr has a healthy amount of risk baked in: declining enterprise customer counts may scare potential buyers off, and I don't see any near-term catalysts that can help to spark an upward re-rating.

All in all, I'm losing patience with this stock: it's best to lock in losses here and invest elsewhere.

Q3 download

Let's now go through Sprinklr's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Sprinklr's revenue grew 9% y/y to $219.1 million, which came in meaningfully ahead of Wall Street's weaker expectations of $209.6 million (+4% y/y). However, we note that a large rise in professional services revenue (+44% y/y) contributed to a lot of the quarter's growth. While we note this is a good indicator for new client onboardings, Sprinklr performs professional services work essentially at cost ($28.8 million of revenue, offset by $28.2 million of gross cost): its long-term scalability rests on its ability to grow its subscription base. On this front, trends have been choppier: subscription revenue of $190.3 million grew at a weaker 5% y/y pace, and decelerated a point from 6% growth in Q2.

Where we are most disappointed is that Sprinklr's upmarket sales strategy appears to be losing tailwinds. As shown in the chart below, the company now has only 145 customers who generate more than $1 million in ARR: a decline of two such customers year over year.

Another poor indicator: while the company's current RPO (backlog that will be recognized as revenue in the next 12 months) grew 3% y/y to $562.2 million, this was slower than overall subscription revenue growth. Furthermore, overall RPO (including longer-term backlog) actually fell -5% y/y to $857.6 million, and RPO balances have actually been in sequential decline every quarter for the past year:

To me, this is a signal that customers are unwilling to sign up for long-term commitments in Sprinklr. This may be a reflection of a weaker macro environment, where companies are pulling back on growth investments in sales and marketing: which impacts the kind of CX research and activities that Sprinklr promotes. Sprinklr's Employee Experience (EX) products could also be getting hit by waning corporate headcount, as companies look to streamline teams and leverage AI to perform more tasks.

Furthermore, Sprinklr's net revenue retention rates clocked in at just 102% in the quarter, which indicates only 2% net expansion: not really a ringing endorsement for Sprinklr's success at "land and expand" into larger customers who are able to purchase multiple products and onboard more teams onto the Sprinklr platform.

On the profitability front, we note that Sprinklr's pro forma operating margins still saw 4 points of y/y improvement to 15%:

Still, 5% subscription revenue growth on top of ~15% pro forma operating margins isn't exactly a cause for celebration, when even many traditional "Rule of 40" companies like HubSpot (HUBS) and Workday (WDAY) are trading down for the year and at reasonably cheap valuations in the 5-6x revenue range.

Key takeaways

Sprinklr is looking increasingly like a value trap as its subscription growth rate wanes and the company shows signs of churn among its larger enterprise customer base. Right now, there are many stocks on sale in the software sector: I'd rather pay a slightly higher multiple for a higher-quality, beaten-down stock like HubSpot or Atlassian than chase extreme value in a company like Sprinklr of more questionable quality.

data.title: Tenet Healthcare's Surge Isn't Over Yet
data.description:
One of my best calls in recent years has been Tenet Healthcare Corporation (THC). This is a fascinating business, with ownership over 50 hospitals and a growing ambulatory care business. In my latest article about the company, published in October of 2023, I found myself impressed with its growth and valuation. This led me to reaffirm it as a ‘buy’ candidate. And since then, things have gone quite well. The stock is up 288%, which is massively higher than the 58.3% rise that the S&P 500 experienced. Since my original ‘buy’ rating on it in February of the previous year, things have not gone quite as well, but they are still going great. The stock is up to 100.4% since then, which is nearly four times the 52.9% rise that the broader market enjoyed.

You would think that, after such monumental upside, it would be time for a downgrade. To be clear, I do think that there are certain risks facing the company, specifically on the regulatory side of things. And this does warrant some consideration. However, as things stand, shares look incredibly cheap, both on an absolute basis and relative to other similar firms. And with management even admitting that the regulatory exposure is not as great as it might first initially appear, I think that this still makes for a compelling ‘buy’ candidate.

Registering with Tenet Healthcare Corporation

Whenever I go a while without analyzing a company, I'd like to go back to the foundation and lay out exactly what the company does and how it operates. The way I view it, if I haven't looked at the business in a while, other investors might not have either. Fundamentally speaking, this is a fascinating business. Management describes it as a diversified healthcare services firm that's based out of Texas. It also has operations in the Philippines, but overwhelmingly, this is a US based firm.

It has, at this moment, two different operating segments. The first and largest of these is its Hospital Operations segment, which owns 50 acute care and specialty hospitals. It boasts a network of employed positions, over 100 outpatient facilities that include urgent care centers, imaging centers, off campus hospital emergency departments, and even what it calls micro hospitals. In essence, this part of the company generates revenue by providing various health care services to customers. Examples here include checkups, surgeries, consultations, and the like.

Even more impressive, to me, is the smaller Ambulatory Care division. This part of the company, which management often refers to as USPI, holds indirect ownership in hundreds of interests and 530 ambulatory surgery centers. 398 of these are consolidated, with the remaining falling under joint ventures or minority interests. He also has 26 surgical hospitals here, with eight of those being consolidated. This is a national company, with a presence in 37 states, which is far greater than the eight states that its Hospital Operations segment has a presence in. Through this unit, it offers up various procedures and services such as those involving orthopedics, total joint replacement services, spinal and other musculoskeletal procedures, gastroenterology services, pain management services, urology services, and the like.

Over the last few years now, Tenet Healthcare Corporation has seen some consistent growth on the top line. Revenue inched up from $19.17 billion in 2022 to $20.67 billion in 2024. Profitability shot up from $411 million to $3.20 billion. But honestly, this surge on the bottom line is deceptive. I say this because, in 2024, the company booked a $2.92 billion gain on some of its hospitals. Leading up to that point, the company had 61 hospitals. But in 2024, it brought in $4.98 billion on a gross basis from the sale of facilities and other assets. Other profitability metrics are more meaningful. As the chart above illustrates, cash flows for the company have moved generally higher, though 2024 did see some weakness on an operating cash flow basis compared to what the business saw the previous year.

In the chart above, you can also see results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. Revenue and cash flows grew across the board. This comes not only as a result of a rise in the number of hospitals that the business has in operation from 49 to 50, but also from some other changes that the company has exhibited. But I will get to that in just a moment.

First, I think that a general description of the performance of its Hospital Operations segment is in order. From 2022 through 2024, revenue associated with this business dipped from $16.12 billion to $16.14 billion. And for this year, the $12.04 billion generated during the first nine months fell short of the $12.33 billion the company reported a year ago. The general weakness that the company has seen during this window of time can be chalked up primarily to the aforementioned asset sale.

In order to better position itself for the future, the company sacrificed near-term revenue. And as a result, the decrease in the size of its physical footprint translated to a decline in overall admissions. Last year, for instance, admissions came in at only 505,828. That's down from the 538,324 reported a year earlier. And adjusted admissions dropped from 983,914 to 914,479. We have seen some recovery in overall admissions for the year. Also, it is worth noting that surgeries have declined as a result of the changes that the company has made to its portfolio.

Last year, surgeries amounted to 307,732. But the year before that, they were 347,156. For this year, the 202,321 surgeries reported by the company fell short of the 204,013 reported last year. Perhaps the only improvement that we saw across the board has been a rise in the utilization rate of its licensed beds. As these declined, and as the company focused on the assets that it decided to keep, the utilization rate expanded from 48.9% in 2022 to 49.6% in 2024. And for the first nine months of this year, the 51.2% utilization rate exceeded the 51% reported a year ago.

Although the Hospital Operations segment for the company has seen revenue drop some, profitability has been on the rise. But the real driver of upside, both from a profitability standpoint and from a revenue standpoint, has been its Ambulatory Care division. Management has made a concerted effort to grow this part of the company. And there's a very good reason why.

Simply put, this part of the business has significant profit margins. As the chart above illustrates, revenue exploded from $3.25 billion in 2022 to $4.53 billion last year. And with that, segment profits jumped from $1.33 billion to $1.81 billion. For the first nine months of this year, the $3.74 billion in revenue, which exceeded the $3.28 billion reported for last year, yielded profits of $1.45 billion. That was up soundly from the $1.28 billion reported a year earlier.

As the chart above illustrates, the company has invested heavily in growing the physical footprint for this part of the business. Back in 2022, the company had only 308 consolidated facilities under this unit. But today, that number stands at 406. With that rise in location count has also come a growth in the number of consolidated cases that the unit has experienced. More cases mean greater revenue, so this kind of expansion is perfectly logical.

Honestly, I am very excited about this part of the business. And that's because management has demonstrated, not only through revenue and profit growth but also through margin growth, that this part of the business is the real driver of value for shareholders. Of the $4.47 billion to $4.57 billion worth of EBITDA the company is forecasting for this year, nearly half of that is expected to come from the Ambulatory Care segment despite the fact that it will be responsible for only about 24.1% of overall revenue. And long-term, the company continues to take advantage of service line additions under this division. In fact, for the first nine months of 2025, it added 211 new service lines. This has helped to fuel same-facility system-wide revenue growth. From 2015 through 2025, the annualized growth rate of same-facility system-wide revenue was about 6.2%.

Simultaneous to capturing this kind of expansion, the company has also been focused on reducing overall leverage. Back in 2017, for instance, the net leverage ratio for the company was 5.86. But through organic growth, growth by means of acquisitions, and the aforementioned hospital asset sale, the firm ultimately reduced this to 2.54 by the end of last year. And as of the third quarter of this year, it stood at 2.30. When we strip out non-controlling interests, we get a decline from 2017 through today from 6.95 to 2.93. And what's really impressive to me is that management has accomplished this while also returning capital to shareholders. In fact, in the first nine months of this year, it has bought back $1.19 billion worth of stock. And with $1.69 billion in buyback capacity remaining, we can expect additional purchases as time goes on.

If we project out results based on management's expectations for this year, and if we also use historical results for the company for the 2024 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above how shares of the business are currently priced. Honestly, these are very low multiples. And this is true not only on an absolute basis but relative to other similar firms also. In that same chart, I compared Tenet Healthcare Corporation to five other similar firms. And what I found was that, across the board, our candidate was cheaper than these other five businesses.

To illustrate for you just how much potential upside might exist here, I need only point you to the chart above. In it, you can see how much upside the stock would experience if it were to trade at the multiple of the lowest of these five competitors. Using the price to operating cash flow approach, we would see upside of 41.8%. And using the EV to EBITDA approach, it would be a more modest 10.2%. And if we use, instead, the average of these five businesses, stripping out the most expensive of the five companies when it comes to the price to operating cash flow multiple since it does appear to be a clear outlier, we would get upside of between 75.9% and 196.5%.

Probably the only thing that I am a bit cautious about when it comes to Tenet Healthcare Corporation would be exposure to changes in the regulatory environment. Unless there are changes that are made, millions of Americans are going to lose their health insurance because of the government spending bill that was passed earlier this year. Medicare will likely be spared from this. But over time, we are looking at a 15% total reduction in federal Medicaid funding thanks to the current administration. In fact, one source estimates that by 2034, as many as 7.5 million people could lose Medicaid coverage compared to if the bill had not been passed. And another 2.1 million will lose access because of the expiration of certain ACA marketplace subsidies.

We don't know the full impact that this will have on a company such as Tenet Healthcare Corporation. But we do know that about 11.7% of the firm's revenue in the latest quarter came from Medicaid. A lot of the company’s money comes from managed care activities. But we know that, included in that, would be those covered under the marketplace. The good news is that only about 7% of consolidated revenue that the business generates comes from those exchanges. But still, this could have some impact on the company even though management has not laid out its own expectations regarding potential pain. The fact that the business is so cheap, however, and the fact that only a sizable minority portion of its revenue comes from sources that we'll see only some reduction in care, makes me feel more confident about the position of the company even in light of this challenge. But investors should absolutely keep an eye on things moving forward.

Takeaway

Fundamentally speaking, I would argue that Tenet Healthcare Corporation remains a compelling prospect. Admittedly, there are certain risk factors, as I laid out already. But on the whole, this seems to be a healthy company that's trading on the cheap. Management has made great progress in recent years, and that has been reflected in the form of higher revenue, profits, cash flows, and an elevated share price. But still, I think that additional upside is warranted here. So naturally, I have no problem maintaining my ‘buy’ rating on the stock.

data.title: Home Depot: Likely Not A Home Run Pick -- Sell
data.description:
Intro

My last article about Home Depot (HD) went online on 21 June 2025. I was not optimistic for both, the underlying business as well as the stock’s future performance. After almost half a year, the stock is basically trading on the same level like at the time of analysis.

My sell rating so far has not played out like I assumed it would. However, I’d like to point out that HD stock is showing weakness in a market that is close to its highs. HD stock recently made a new low for the year and is down 16% YTD as of writing.

In this update, I am discussing the latest Q3 results (release + call), which confirmed my negative view, strengthening my bearish rating which I am keeping up. Sell HD stock due to high valuation, but weak immediate prospects.

Not nailed it yet, but ready to be hammered down

Home Depot is known and respected for its long-term outperformance against the S&P 500. But on a shorter time frame, HD has lost much of its drive.

The chart below shows HD stock trailing the broader index by most 30 percentage points over the last twelve months. The divergence started about with the dawn of tariffs. A second big hit occurred this fall, sending HD stock to a new low for the year.

The reason: the underlying business remains challenged. Q3 2025 results were lackluster and the outlook lacked positive catalysts to initiate a turnaround.

Home Depot reported Q3 sales of $41.4 billion, which were up 2.8% from the prior year. However, this sounds better than it actually was as comparable sales rose only 0.2%, with U.S. comparable sales even just being up by an abysmal +0.1% — practically flat as I like to say.

The reason for the difference is the closed acquisition of GMS to strengthen the professional segment, which contributed inorganic sales growth over approximately eight weeks in the quarter.

Digital sales increased above-average at 11%, while retail sales were much weaker. Pictured and framed below, comparable and total customer transactions were both down with –1.6% and –1.4%, respectively. The current consumer environment is dominated by uncertainty, leading to fewer discretionary purchases.

Saving the top line from falling into negative territory were higher average purchases (tickets), pointing towards modest price increases which management confirmed during the call. But this was only part of the equation, as higher average ticket was also driven by bigger transactions over $1000 which pulled up the average and grew by 2.3%.

This means broader growth is lacking which is not supportive down the road.

Reading past the headline, cracks under the hood become apparent. The quarter was dominated by challenges. Management citied a complete absence of storm activity in 2025 so far. Of course this is not something the company can control as it’s entirely random. But it has been a supportive factor last year. The (fortunate) lack of significant hurricane created the largest sales shortfall in the business, with repair activities being seen as muted.

As a result and first sneak peak into Q4 during the call, October (Hurricane Milton in 2024) comparable sales turned negative with –1.5%.

Management also talked about housing turnover sitting at 40-year lows, home-price declines having spread to additional markets, and broader consumer uncertainty weighing heavily on larger discretionary projects. In brief, only necessary purchases are being made, with nice-to-have investments being postponed.

With the hoped-for recovery taking longer than initially expected amid weak core business activity, management lowered full-year 2025 guidance. Total sales growth is now expected at approximately 3% (including roughly $2 billion from GMS), comparable sales only slightly positive, and adjusted operating margin around 13% (40 basis-point reduction from prior guidance).

Adjusted earnings per share are projected to decline stronger than expected by 5% when comparing 52 weeks this year to 53 weeks in 2024.

With the presentation of the Q2 results in August 2025, the targets were to achieve comparable sales of 1% and adjusted EPS of –3% — a clear acceleration to the downside, offering an explanation why HD stock made a new low post Q3 results.

To sum it up, Home Depot continues to face a storm of cautious consumers, holding back discretionary purchases. While management believes it is gaining market share across both retail and professional channels (and plans to do so in all phases of the cycle), my main issue is the stock price.

While a cyclical business like Home Depot can have weaker quarters, the valuation should reflect that, especially with a lowered guidance and the absence of positive catalysts. The combination of persistent housing market weakness and cautious consumer behavior points to an at the minimum challenging fourth quarter. Likely even an spillover into next year.

Not counting net debt of over $50 billion, the price to sales ratio continues to point towards a still rich valuation with high downside risks, should consumer and housing weakness persists well into next year.

I have chosen the PS ratio because it fluctuates less than earnings and cash flows do for such a cyclical business like Home Depot.

But of course, let’s have a look at FCF metrics, too. FCF over the last twelve months was ~$14 billion. With an EV of a smidgen above $400 billion, we have an EV / FCF of about 29x. This is decisively too high and too risky.

The dividend yields less than 3% with two-thirds of FCF being paid out — not attractive either to compensate for waiting.

That’s why HD stock remains a sell in my view. The operational risks are tilted to the downside, but the stock’s valuation does not reflect that. The risk and reward is strongly negative with little upside, but considerable downside.

Risks to my Thesis

A meaningful consumer and housing recovery would reignite growth, justifying the current stock price. My sell rating could prove to be too pessimistic then.

A bit related to the first point, significantly lower interest rates would make borrowing cheaper, potentially incentivizing discretionary spending in the housing sector.

Conclusion

The recent quarterly results showed weak underlying performance and culminated in a unsatisfying outlook. Given the lack of positive catalysts, mixed with high valuation multiples, the risk and reward is tilted strongly to the downside — SELL.

data.title: Tourmaline Oil: There's Deep Value In Canada's Top Natural Gas Producer
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I first covered Tourmaline Oil (OTCPK:TRMLF)(TOU:CA), I rated them a Strong Buy thanks to their deep discount despite strong growth and expansion plans that solidify its position as the number one natural gas producer in Canada.

Despite weakness in the market seen during their latest reporting period, Tourmaline continued to report solid numbers and is ready to adapt to the environment if it has to, while the long-term should continue to go in the commodity’s favor thanks to long-term tailwinds and a shifting energy landscape.

Internal Developments

Tourmaline Oil’s Q3 report showed strong improvements despite the lowest local gas prices in 30 years, with CAD 79.51 million during the quarter and CAD 582.61 million during the first 9 months of 2025, but with massive CAPEX continuing as they’re investing in their major multi-year expansion in Northeast British Columbia, with key infrastructure in preparation for LNG Canada.

At current strip pricing, the company expects operating cash flows of roughly CAD 4 billion and free cash flow of approximately CAD 0.9 billion in 2026. Meanwhile, during their Q3 Earnings Release, they also highlighted their ability to significantly cut costs if the environment warrants it, while also highlighting that these cost cuts would primarily be drilling-related:

And for every USD 0.10 per Mcf that AECO basis tightens, our '26 cash flow and free cash flow would increase by approximately $50 million. And should natural gas prices weaken in 2026, we certainly have the option to reduce capital spending as appropriate to optimize free cash flow and our planned shareholder returns. Approximately $200 million to $250 million of currently planned capital spending could be deferred in such a low price scenario, and that would really have only a minor impact on '26 production guidance.

During their latest call, Tourmaline Oil’s CEO also mentioned exploring the potential sale of their Peace River High complex, which can save costs and generate an influx of capital (expected price not disclosed yet, of course):

We have elected to pursue the potential sale of our Peace River High light oil and gas complex, so the Charlie Lake Play, which we actually pioneered back in Duvernay Oil Corp days. If completed, this sale would further lower corporate OpEx and provide proceeds that could be reinvested into our higher-margin BC growth assets or emerging EP opportunities that we've assembled in the Deep Basin. So this initiative is just a subset of the significant internal value creation opportunities that exist within the company's overall portfolio.

As a note, they also mentioned that their exploration program has generated opportunities that could “fully replace” the volumes they’d lose from the Peace River High complex in 2 or 3 years.

They also adjusted their special dividend recently to CAD 0.25, with the CEO giving a very good answer that I’m actually very happy to see and I see as a plus:

(...) we do not intend to use the balance sheet to fund special dividends. I think having 2 quarters of the lowest AECO prices in 30 years is a rare circumstance. And for Q3, paying the special using the balance sheet was one of those rare circumstances. But we will continue to look at the growth capital and the special dividend potential and find that balance.

Financially, based on Tourmaline Oil’s latest report, we continue to see an okay position, with the current portion of debt and lower current assets weakening this, for now (note that everything is in CAD).

Still, the long-term debt is low for the company’s size, and they also mentioned the possibility of debt reductions to adapt to the environment, with moves like the Topaz share sale (about CAD 300 million; CAD 230 million completed on October 28, as mentioned in the Q3 call) and the potential Peace River High complex sale helping in this regard.

Macro Developments

US uncertainty continued, and although this company is Canadian, we can’t ignore the importance of the US in the global natural gas or LNG markets, even if we’re talking about different local prices, infrastructure, policies, etc. As such, these rate cuts can positively influence the country’s role in global natural gas or LNG markets, potentially leading to more demand for Tourmaline’s gas from the US, as Tourmaline strives to sell as much gas outside of its local market as possible.

This came with some fear due to the US Government going through its longest shutdown in history, but we’ve seen Fed officials recently boosting the market’s estimates.

This is important because rate cuts could help fuel the country’s demand for oil and gas, while also improving investments in LNG terminals and related infrastructure, as the US ultimately aims (and succeeded so far) at replacing Europe’s dependency on Russian gas, with Trump’s $750 billion US-EU deal reinforcing and basically securing what was already clear.

The recent news about a potential Ukraine peace agreement can turn out to be a “negative” for the oil and gas industry, as that would potentially allow Russia to diminish the impact of sanctions (recent US sanctions were very impactful on a global scale) and allow it to focus once again on commodity production.

This comes as a new draft proposed by the US was mostly agreed by Ukraine, with only some “minor” details left to settle. It is very likely not the end - and we’ve seen a big attack coming from Russia the day after this news came - but it’s a step in that direction.

Sure, I really don’t see their production level getting to the pre-war era or Russian gas flowing back in Europe at the same rates, but any extra gas on the global market shifts the dynamics and can influence prices. For example, if China or India continue to benefit from cheap Russian gas, that means there’s less demand for it on international markets, especially as these countries depend on LNG imports and China is the largest importer of LNG (recently surpassing Japan).

OPEC’s recent moves can also be important for natural gas, as we’re ultimately talking about commodities that are often closely tied together in terms of production. All these things are tied together, and we’ve recently seen OPEC and its allies agreeing to boost their oil production in December, but decided to halt their increases in Q1 2026 at least (until March), which can be a very significant boost to oil and even gas prices going forward, as the lower supply could boost them.

It’s also worth mentioning that OPEC’s production increases combined with tariff uncertainty and other macro pressures were key in oil prices falling this year, while natural gas rose after a couple of weak years and even skyrocketed recently as a result of a mix of factors including weather/seasonal-related demand growth, reduced pipeline flows, tighter supply, and more.

Still, natural gas demand is expected to reach a new all-time high in 2026, and I believe this trend should continue over time as the “Third Wave” of LNG growth ramps up, backed by a shifting energy landscape and more demand from AI or data centers, for example.

This comes as many of the key LNG projects are ramping up or under construction and are expected to be complete over the following couple of years, many of which came as an almost immediate response to Russia’s initial attack on Ukraine in early 2022, being a key aspect that led to the US becoming the largest LNG exporter in the world.

But again, just because the long-term seems to be in favor of Tourmaline and natural gas, we’ve still got the classic risks associated with these commodities and their dependency on Canada, local prices, infrastructure that needs improvements, AECO gas prices trading at steep discounts, etc. You know, it doesn’t matter if HH (the US) or TTF (Europe) or any other gas price skyrockets if you can't get your gas there.

Still, I’ll also highlight the counter-arguments here, as Tourmaline is arguably one of the best-run natural gas companies on the planet, with plenty of experience in managing these risks with aggressive hedges above AECO prices, processing plants that help them to avoid supply bottlenecks, investing in infrastructure combined with the LNG Canada project and other US/Asian market contracts, to the point where Tourmaline is more of an exporter than local seller, with a major part of their production being physically transported to US hubs or sold to international LNG buyers.

Updated Valuation

For Tourmaline Oil’s valuation, I'll assume $550 million in FCF in 2025 (everything here is in USD), $650 million in 2026 and 2027 and $1.5 billion in 2028 (still not much higher than previous levels), and then growing at a 4% CAGR for the subsequent years until year 10 thanks to the solid demand for natural gas and LNG, as well as their project ramp-ups, with a 2.5% terminal growth rate - well below their expectations. I'll go for a WACC of 8% for a better margin of safety, with $1.27 billion in net debt, as well as 386.98 million shares outstanding as of the Q3 report. This gets us to an enterprise value of $24.88 billion, an equity value of $23.61 billion and an intrinsic value of $61.01 for TRMLF and CAD 85.26 for TOU:CA.

Conclusions

Tourmaline Oil remains a Strong Buy in my opinion, offering very solid exposure to a vital commodity that should play a key role in our future, with strong financials and good management on top.

At the end of the day, it’s very hard to beat their quality and low-cost production, which is not only the highest in Canada, but is also managed by its founder - which isn’t that common in oil and gas anymore - with founder and CEO Mike Rose still running the business in a good direction in my opinion, while also having multiple decades of Tier 1 inventory available.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Standing Repo Facility Watch - Is The Fed's Ceiling Tool Working?
data.description:
Standing Repo Facility Usage (Late November, 2025):

US banks and primary dealers have ramped up usage of the Standing Repo Facility in the last week of November, ending the month with a USD 24 billion usage of the lending facility.

Repo rates spiked toward the end of the week, with SOFR and the Tri-party General Collateral Rate (TGCR) reaching 4.12% and 4.08% on Friday, respectively, breaching the Federal Reserve’s overnight interest rate target range.

Repo storm hit again as rate surges at November-end

Key Implications: This is the second month-end in a row the Fed saw repo rate surge past its federal funds rate target range. December 1 is also the day the Fed ended its quantitative tightening program, which should help ease liquidity strain in the market in general.

What is the Standing Repo Facility?

The Standing Repo Facility (SRF) is the Federal Reserve’s permanent “ceiling tool.” Established in July 2021, it allows Primary Dealers and eligible banks to borrow cash overnight from the Fed by offering safe assets such as US Treasuries and agency mortgage-backed securities as collateral.

The Specs

  • The Rate: Priced at the top of the Fed Funds target range, acting as an interest rate ceiling tool.
  • The Limit: Capped at $500 billion per day.
  • The Goal: To prevent a repeat of the 2019 repo crisis by ensuring cash is always available during stress events.

Why the SRF isn’t Fixing the “Leaky Ceiling”

The fact that Standing Repo Facility once again failed to put a cap on the money market interest rates showed that the so-called “leaky ceiling” problem continues to haunt the Fed.

Should the Fed be more concerned that its interest rate ceiling tool failed to put a lid on the repo rates?

Looking through the period-end surges

Period-end surges in repo rates are generally regarded as “tolerable” by the Fed. This is because the main culprit during such periods is well-known.

Dealers usually find their balance sheet capacity limited by regulatory factors, so they reduce their participation in the repo transactions, generating volatility in the repo rates.

As these period-end fluctuations more often than not reflect regulatory limitations instead of a genuine market-wide liquidity shortfall, the Fed has some leeway to look through these effects.

Repo rates are not the Fed’s official target

As Roberto Perli, manager of the New York Fed’s System Open Market Account, said in a mid-November speech, repo rate surges are “not concerning” because the target range is defined in terms of the federal funds rate (FFR). As long as the effective FFR is below the US central bank’s upper limit, the Fed should not panic.

It’s only when the repo rates stay “persistently or substantially above the top of the target range” that the situation would become problematic, as “they could pull up the EFFR and pose difficulties for rate control,” Perli explained.

It’s a liquidity backstop and it carries stigma

SRF is designed as a liquidity backstop, instead of a liquidity provision tool. As New York Fed President John Williams emphasized, banks should not rely on this facility as a source of liquidity day-to-day. Hence, the SRF is supposed to be quiet on “normal” days; but its usage should shoot up when there are temporary liquidity strains in the system, much like the usage pattern during November.

This usage pattern, nonetheless, can breed a stigma problem, where financial institutions would become wary of sourcing liquidity from the Fed tool as “unusual” usage would be considered a sign of weakness. Banks tend to avoid any association with such signals at all costs, as loss of confidence can destroy a bank in an instant.

Even though only overall SRF usage - instead of the amount an individual financial firm lends - is disclosed daily, primary dealers in a mid-November behind-the-door meeting told the New York Fed that they are reluctant to use SRF because they consider “borrowing directly from the central bank still carries a stigma and could be seen as a sign of trouble,” as Bloomberg reported.

What Next?

The Fed is planning on easing the month-end repo rate surge by introducing central clearing in SRF transactions. Central clearing can help free up dealers’ balance sheet capacity to lend from the SRF.

As explained in the October meeting minutes, “[c]entral clearing could increase counterparties’ willingness to use the SRF when there is upward pressure on repo rates, which would damp pressures on the federal funds rate.”

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: 2026 Global Investment Outlooks
data.description:
By William Davies, Nicolas Janvier, CFA, Neil Robson, Gene Tannuzzo, CFA, and Ed Al-Hussainy

Introduction

After a resilient 2025 marked by tariff uncertainty and cooling inflation, the global economy enters 2026 delicately balanced. Economic growth has proven surprisingly durable, and markets have continued to climb. However, high government deficits and diverging policy paths remain key risks. Global growth is expected to moderate, and interest rates are likely to stabilize at lower levels. Investors face markets with the scope for further appreciation but the factors driving performance will differ across regions, sectors and asset classes.

Read our latest macroeconomic, equity and fixed-income insights to learn where our team of investment professionals believes the opportunities - and challenges - are likely to emerge in 2026.

Macroeconomic Outlook

Treading a finer line

Resilient growth and rising markets mask underlying structural tensions - and the risks of a misstep are accumulating. We assess the balance for investors.

As we look ahead to 2026, the global economy is walking an ever-finer line. Growth has proven surprisingly durable, inflation has moderated (albeit unevenly) and markets have continued to climb. But beneath the surface, imbalances are building. We believe the coming year will be defined by how successfully policymakers and investors can navigate the narrowing path.

Resilient progress, unexpected route

2025 broadly delivered what many expected: higher equities, gradual rate cuts and contained inflation. Yet it was not necessarily achieved in the way forecasters imagined.

Corporate earnings growth in the U.S. was less than expected but nonetheless resilient and particularly strong in technology, and durable consumer demand sustained growth - even as inflationary pressures persisted.

However, the divergence between regions has widened. Inflation sits near 2% in the eurozone, closer to 3% in the U.S. and nearly at 4% in the U.K. These differences reflect not only domestic policy approaches but also shifting global dynamics - most notably the principal story of 2025, the emergence of tariffs. As a result, the policy risks facing central banks have become more complex and the margin for error smaller.

Tariffs and inflation: a new kind of supply shock

We see today’s inflationary environment as fundamentally different to the post-COVID surge. Post-pandemic, it was driven by excess demand and supply constraints as economies reopened; now it is shaped by supply constraints linked to trade policy and geopolitical uncertainty.

While some economists argue that tariffs are a one-off price adjustment, it is possible they are more likely to feed through to sustained inflationary pressure in 2026. Higher import costs tend to translate into higher wage demands and pricing power through the supply chain. Tariffs have not only raised costs directly, but their broader effect has been to disrupt supply chains and delay corporate decision making. Companies initially absorbed some of these costs; we believe more pass-through lies ahead, though not dollar-for-dollar. In the U.S., where tariffs are most pronounced, inflation is proving sticky at around 3%. Conversely, Europe is seeing a disinflationary impulse as Chinese exports, directed away from the U.S., bring cheaper goods to Europe.

For investors, this implies a more fragmented global inflation picture as we move forward - and therefore greater divergence in monetary policy and currency movements.

Central banks under pressure

Importantly, central banks continue to operate independently. However, that status is being tested. With President Trump signaling that he would prefer rates closer to 1% than 4%, and with Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell’s term ending in May 2026, the Fed faces continued political scrutiny. A shift toward politically aligned appointments may compromise its long-term focus on price stability. Investors should remain vigilant and consider the implications for inflation expectations and asset pricing.

In addition, the government debt story is closer to becoming a market constraint. The U.S. is on course to exceed 130% debt-to-GDP by the end of the decade, while France is projected to reach 118%, with its deficit stubbornly above 5% of GDP. When confidence erodes, repricing can be swift. The fact that 10-year yields in France exceed those of Italy and Spain - once the focus of concern during the euro crisis in 2009 - highlights how quickly investors can reassess financial risk, even within developed markets. Indeed, the U.K.’s mapped path to deficit reduction is, while ultimately stabilizing, painful and problematic, illustrative of the difficulties in attempting to solve this problem.

With increasing levels of government debt, it is possible that a funding scare in one major economy could raise the cost of borrowing across others.

Therefore, we believe the risk of policy error - specifically, cutting rates too far too fast - is rising. Lowering short-term rates to ease financial strains could steepen yield curves sharply if bond investors lose confidence in inflation control, raising the five- to 10-year funding cost and blunting any short-rate relief. The experience of early 2025, when reciprocal tariffs briefly destabilized U.S. bond markets, underlines that risk. In such an environment, with deficits high and pressures building on all sides, bond markets continue to act as a disciplining force - on governments and central banks alike.

With an expectation of wider dispersion in growth, employment, inflation and deficits across major economies in 2026, there are opportunities to diversify interest rate exposure and protect portfolios against equity drawdowns or a sharp deterioration in employment.

Global trade in transition

Tariffs and political uncertainty have altered the logic of globalization. Companies that once expanded freely across geographies now face incentives to “friend-shore” production or invest domestically. The absence of stability around trade rules has led many CEOs to simply delay investment decisions. We expect this uncertainty to persist and suspect now that tariffs have been introduced, unwinding them will be difficult.

As a result, emerging markets (EM) are feeling both headwinds and opportunities. A weaker U.S. dollar has eased pressure on external debt, but the largest EM economies - China and India - face some of the largest tariff restrictions at 47% and 50%, respectively. However, they both benefit from lower GDP per capita, which leaves ample runway for domestic growth. We believe selective exposure within EMs is warranted, with a focus on those benefiting from new supply-chain realignments and competitive currencies.

AI and energy: themes in motion

The rapid advance of artificial intelligence (AI) is another topic dominating discourse, corporate strategy and market sentiment. We believe AI investment remains at the early-adoption stage - marked by extraordinary potential and clear signs of fiscal excess. Circularity is a concern with firms investing in their own suppliers and partners, blurring financial exposures and creating dependencies. This is workable when there is momentum, fragile when there isn’t. Our credit analysts are scrutinizing such structures closely. There are echoes of the dot-com boom of the early 2000s, with some companies generating immense cashflows from selling the “picks and shovels” of AI, while others spend heavily in the hope of future rewards. Well-capitalized businesses are better placed to fund this long gestation period.

The energy transition is another enduring theme. While we acknowledge that the rollback of the U.S. Inflation Reduction Act has slowed momentum in the U.S., global investment in renewables, electrification and grid infrastructure is set to continue. So far, $2.2 trillion of capital has been allocated to renewables, nuclear, grids, storage, low-emissions fuels, efficiency and electrification in 2025 - twice as much as the $1.1 trillion going to oil, natural gas and coal.1 In Europe and parts of Asia, policy support and corporate commitment remain strong. Consequently, we still expect energy transition to be a persistent source of capital market opportunity, even if progress is likely to be more uneven.

Three-dimensional investment thinking

After another strong year for equities, valuations - particularly in the U.S. - leave less margin for error. Market reactions to geopolitical shocks and tariff announcements have shown how quickly corrections can occur and reverse. But if we were to see a downturn alongside weaker growth or rising unemployment, the rebound might not be so swift or profound.

Diversification, therefore, is non-negotiable. Investors should think in three dimensions: across asset classes (equities, credit and alternatives); across regions (the U.S., Europe and EMs); and across themes (AI, fiscal resilience, the energy transition, etc). Credit markets could provide early indications of shifting dynamics, highlighting any increased differentiation between higher and lower quality borrowers. Private equity, meanwhile, could face headwinds from higher borrowing costs and tighter liquidity.

1 IEA, World Energy Investment 2025, June 2025.

All data in the article is sourced from Bloomberg, as of October 31, 2025, unless otherwise noted.

The bottom line

The global economy enters 2026 in reasonable health, but the risks of a misstep are accumulating. Inflation remains sticky and uneven, fiscal deficits are uncomfortably high and seemingly without solution, and the geopolitical framework continues to creak. For policymakers and investors alike, the balance between caution and optimism has rarely been so delicate.

We believe driving portfolio growth in this environment will come from patience, discipline, diversification and selectivity - with an active approach best placed to recognize where change creates opportunity and exuberance masks fragility. The path is narrow, but it still offers a route to positive outcomes.

Equity Outlook

Harnessing growth with a broad view

We maintain a constructive outlook for equities, with a broadening of opportunities for selective investment, backed by disciplined diversification.

Today’s mix of policy easing, supportive fundamental backdrop and transformative AI investments sets a constructive outlook for equity markets in 2026. Economic growth is steady, and central banks stand poised for further rate cuts as inflation moderates. The continued buildout of the artificial intelligence (AI) supply chain is driving momentum across a broadening set of beneficiaries. Additionally, fiscal expansion in Europe and structural changes in Japan stand to buoy earnings growth from a global perspective. Despite this broad-based optimism, we remain cognizant of potential risks and view diversification as essential, particularly as a broader set of opportunities emerges.

Earnings’ prospects drive positive outlook

U.S. company earnings will likely be a key driver of equity returns in 2026. Our expectations are for robust growth with the likelihood of upside surprises exceeding the potential for disappointment. Our base case is for high single-digit gains with scope for low double-digits at the upper end of our forecasts.

Several factors contribute to our positive assessment:

  • Companies appear to have adjusted well to the new environment of higher tariffs and resulting cost shifts with redesign, alternative sourcing and selective pricing curbing earnings headwinds to an estimated 3%–5% range. Gross margins could surprise positively if inflation remains contained.
  • The AI-driven capital expenditure cycle persists as a potent force, and we expect it to be stimulative for revenues across a broadening range of sectors.
  • Additionally, interest rates trending lower and inventory buildouts should support earnings recovery in sectors of the economy that have been operating in challenging conditions in recent years. As this occurs, more opportunities will emerge.

We view these trends as supportive of investors’ equity allocations.

Diversification matters - for offense and defense

Diversification always matters, but we view it as a crucial consideration in 2026. We expect continued strength in the U.S., but opportunities are evolving from the era of U.S. exceptionalism. Following the global financial crisis of 2008 (GFC), earnings growth in the U.S. (fueled largely by the technology sector) outstripped the rest of the world. In the years since the pandemic, however, the gap has narrowed significantly. We anticipate that pockets of earnings growth in Europe and Japan will keep pace with the U.S., and a broader range of sectors look capable of delivering appreciation.

Defense and financials are two notable examples. From a market capitalization perspective, we also see firmer support for small-cap stocks. The path of interest rates will be stimulative for companies more closely geared to the economic cycle.

When constructing and monitoring portfolios, we contend that investors should also be wary of hidden concentrations – especially as the AI investment cycle diffuses across a host of industries. Traditional risk models may under-diagnose this trend so investors should think carefully and monitor portfolio balance. At the same time, investors should consider diversifying into areas of the market that were previously out of favor but are once again beginning to generate meaningful earnings growth. For example, we see selective opportunities in areas like life insurance. In addition to generating attractive returns, these broader opportunities can provide an effective counterweight to AI-related thematics. The ‘two markets’ construct should remain at the fore of investor thinking from here.

Global lens - a world of opportunity

April 2025’s tariff announcements prompted a reassessment of geographic exposures and resulting shifts in capital allocations. In 2026, that trend should continue.

  • Europe to accelerate as brakes eased. The prospect of fiscal expansion stoked the fires of interest in European equities during 2025. In 2026 we see this potential being realized. The relaxation of Germany’s debt brake and associated defense and infrastructure spending are set to unlock growth, and lower interest rates also lend support (especially for the periphery states where the region’s prevailing rates are effectively too low). We expect a broadening of performance across sectors and industries, including into areas like financials and industrials. At the same time, we are mindful of risks, especially in countries like France, where political uncertainties cast doubt on economic discipline and the sustainability of high debt levels. In summary, opportunities abound but investors should be attentive to broader developments.
  • Japan’s ongoing transformation. Japan’s economic transformation continues with deflation firmly in the rearview mirror - inflation stands at around 2% and bond yields are above the 3% mark. Ongoing reforms support a more favorable growth environment, and corporate Japan is streamlining balance sheets, embracing a new focus on returns on equity, and investing capital. As with many other developed economies, demographic challenges persist, but for selective investors there are plenty of attractive stock-specific opportunities.

AI capex propels broader impact

The AI-fueled capital expenditure (CapEx) cycle could reach an extraordinary $3.5 trillion through to 2030, primarily through the build-out of data centers and related infrastructure. We view the magnitude of this investment cycle as transformative, with its impact reaching far beyond technology companies as it generates powerful tailwinds for the global economy.

In the U.S., AI capex currently contributes more to GDP growth than traditional consumption. Demand is surging across sectors like semiconductors, industrial equipment, materials, and utilities as investment in AI and AI data centers grows exponentially, driving rising demand for power and water. There are even positive potential impacts for select consumer-facing businesses in locations experiencing concentrated AI-related construction.

Over the next 12–18 months, we expect to gain deeper insight into how firms are monetizing AI within their operations.

Through granular metrics, we aim to assess how management teams are creating or maintaining competitive advantage: How are they innovating product faster than their peers and how are efficiencies impacting future earnings?

Amid all the AI-related optimism it is crucial to remain mindful of associated risks. Valuations are elevated, but with interest rates falling and earnings growth both broadening and accelerating into 2026 it is unlikely we will see a significant correction in valuations. Indeed, the surprise may be that these conditions enable valuations to expand further.

Mindful of medium-term risks

Our six- to 12-month view on equities is constructive, but we are mindful that geopolitics could trigger volatility. Additionally, companies that have invested heavily on AI-related capex will have to start demonstrating a return on that outlay, and bumps along the road towards monetizing AI are likely. Beyond that, medium-term risks are building. Concerns around levels of government debt rank high on our watch list, particularly in

the U.S. and parts of Europe where political fragmentation and fiscal discipline issues are unresolved. Interest payments place a strain on public finances and higher bond yields could trigger a negative reaction in equity markets. We also see structural challenges in demographic trends as aging populations in many developed economies constrain long-term growth potential and alter savings and investment patterns.

The bottom line

We believe that conditions are in place for further appreciation in equity markets in 2026. Growth is sound and interest rates look set on a downward trajectory. These factors, together with increased investment in AI and a broader recovery in corporate profitability, are driving positive momentum. In this environment, we advocate for selective investment and assert that considered diversification is essential. Through intense research, our focus remains on building resilient portfolios to help clients reach their investment outcomes.

Fixed-Income Outlook

Seizing opportunities in a rate-cutting cycle

Fixed-income investors face falling rates, tight spreads and a fragile labor market. The playbook requires locking in yield and managing duration, while staying vigilant on credit quality.

Prospects for bonds in 2026 look strong but not without hurdles. With proactive rate cuts by the Federal Reserve (Fed), resilient corporate fundamentals and continued investor appetite for fixed income, conditions support compelling returns. Still, a weakening labor market and tight credit spreads present potential challenges. We see 2026 shaping up to be a year where investors can find value by locking in yield, managing duration and focusing on diversification - while keeping a close eye on credit quality.

Positioning for proactive Fed rate cuts

We are in an environment where the Fed is proactively cutting rates to remove the risk of negative outcomes, not in reaction to a crisis (i.e., recession). The market is currently pricing in a sequence of cuts totaling 75–100 basis points (BPs) in 2025 and an additional 75 bps in 2026. This suggests the market expects an aggressive cycle of rate cuts, which would be a significant departure from historical norms outside of a recession (see table next page).

To deliver this level of easing, the Fed would require further evidence of labor market deterioration, and this evidence will need to arrive relatively soon. It also requires the Fed to look through (overlook) any direct and secondary effects of tariffs on inflation, the acceleration in private sector capital spending, the easing of financial conditions and the positive fiscal impact of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act.

We believe this scenario is unlikely, which suggests the front end of the Treasury curve is mispriced - creating an opportunity for investors. And unlike previous cycles, where rate cuts steepened the yield curve, today’s cuts are likely to keep the curve stable or slightly flatter. This creates three implications for fixed-income portfolios:

  • Duration becomes attractive. It offers both more yield and downside protection against equity market drawdowns, especially relative to cash.
  • Income harvesting. Investors can capture elevated starting yields without needing to take a directional bet on the economy.
  • Diversification. High-quality fixed income provides a buffer, particularly in an environment where inflation remains relatively subdued.

Our playbook: Finding value with falling rates, tight spreads and strong fundamentals

The tension between labor market softness and ongoing economic growth will define the bond market’s path in 2026. We believe three scenarios could play out:

Fixed-income scenarios

Scenario Description Fixed-income outcomes
Status quo (base case) Labor supply resets lower; unemployment below4.5%; growth remains at 1% Full spectrum multi-sector duration, selective credit
Recession Demand slowdown leads to higher unemployment with a lag Investment grade, longer duration fixed income
Recovery Trade uncertainty recedes; capex leads to hiring; fiscal stimulus; accommodative financial conditions and monetary policy Short-term bonds

Source: Columbia Threadneedle Investments. For illustrative purposes only.

In our base case scenario, with 10-year Treasury yields around 4% and investment-grade credit yielding near 5%, bonds present a compelling value proposition, especially with inflation at around 3%. In this environment, investors should prioritize sectors offering higher yield per unit of duration.

These factors point to specific areas of opportunity within fixed income:

  • Consumer loans. Asset-based finance stands out as an area of value. Backed by healthy household balance sheets and secured with collateral, consumer-oriented bonds offer diversification away from traditional corporate credit.
  • Investment grade. Fundamentals are solid, but elevated prices make corporate bonds less compelling due to the risk of spread widening. For institutional investors, they remain an important asset class for liability matching. Agency mortgage-backed securities offer investment- grade quality at a better value.
  • Artificial intelligence (AI). The massive buildout of AI infrastructure is reshaping credit markets, creating new opportunities for bond investors through innovative funding structures and increased capital demand.
  • International bonds. Non-U.S. growth may begin to look more attractive, with steeper yield curves offering additional risk premium in markets like Japan, France and Australia. There are pockets of value in emerging market debt.
  • Leveraged loans. This is a contrarian call, given tight spreads and falling rates. However, they are not as expensive as other sectors and have matured into a through-the-cycle product, extending the high-yield opportunity set.

At the same time, we remain relatively risk-averse in sectors where we think we are not compensated enough for risk.

Managing portfolio risk in a tight credit environment

There are growing downside risks to the “steady as you go” outlook for 2026:

  • Credit dispersion. Recent high-profile defaults were issuer-specific, reflecting weakness at the bottom end of both household and corporate borrowers. Importantly, we are not seeing that weakness spread. Instead, the real story is dispersion: After several years of monitoring, we are now seeing meaningful differences in credit performance across sectors and issuers.
  • Tariffs. Another potential risk is tariffs. While the economic reaction has been muted so far, the effects may be working through inventories slowly and could hit consumers more forcefully in 2026.
  • Credit volatility. Investors should also be prepared for volatility in spreads as the cycle matures. Focus on real risks - labor market trends, consumer stress, and tariff impacts - while tuning out the noise. This is when and where fundamental credit research shines.
  • Policy and politics. Policy-related noise has been intense and is expected to remain so in 2026. However, investors should keep in mind that this has limited influence on the actual performance of fixed-income markets. Demand for U.S. fixed income continues to be strong, as many policy themes have not significantly impacted market dynamics.

The bottom line

In 2026, we believe the bond market will continue to offer value and opportunity - but not without risk. The Fed’s preemptive cuts, a stable macroeconomic backdrop and healthy demand for fixed income set the stage for constructive returns. Yet, vigilance is warranted as labor market stress and credit events could become more pronounced. By focusing on duration, yield and diversification, investors can position portfolios to weather volatility and capture opportunity in a changing landscape.
