data.title: HYG: Hold For Carry And A Second Leg Higher If Spreads Stay Tight
data.description:
HYG (BlackRock: iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (HYG) )is positioned as an ETF designed to generate steady carry with limited volatility. After reviewing the main characteristics of the fund, our hold thesis is argued by analyzing the situation of credit spreads and reviewing the current situation of the economic cycle.

Fund Overview

The Black Rock iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (HYG), serves as a vehicle to gain exposure to high yield corporate debt in the short-to-intermediate segment of the yield curve (effective duration 2.78 years). The objective of the fund is to replicate the behavior of the Markit iBoxx USD Liquid High Yield Index.

In practice, HYG packages more than a thousand junk bonds into a single vehicle listed on the NYSE Arca, providing the investor with a high coupon flow in exchange for assuming cyclical credit risk.

As they comment in their Summary Prospectus, the strategy is based on representative sampling and aims to replicate the index, not beat it

On the same page (7), the fund states that it will hold at least 80% of its assets in bonds that are part of the index, at least 90% will be fixed income assets of the same type as those in the index, i.e. that additional 10% may be more bonds of the same index or similar HY bonds but which do not have to belong to the index. Finally, it should be noted that the remaining 10% can be allocated to derivatives or fixed income securities that are not of the same type as those in the index.

ETF Dividends

Since 2014, HYG’s dividend yield has been broadly flat, with some swings, around 5.7%.

Similarly, over the past five years, monthly distributions have been consistent with the amounts distributed. At first glance we could mark an average of $0.32 per share (red line), although in the last two years it has distributed slightly higher amounts. In any case,this suggests that the fund is not managed with the goal of growing the dividend, but rather of maintaining a “periodic income” profile that depends on HY credit cycles.

The reliability of the flow is reinforced with the data of “Consistency Grade”, with 17 consecutive years of dividend payments:

Holdings

About half of the portfolio includes holdings that are sensitive to the business cycle (Consumer Cyclical, Communications, Energy, Capital Goods, Technology, etc.) only 16% (Consumer Non-Cyclical and Electric (utilities)) of the holdings’ composition would form the most defensive part and the rest would also tend to behave more like pro-cyclical assets.

Although there is no clear dominant sector, holdings as a whole tend to be more pro-cyclical and as long as the economy is doing well, investors can collect the carry, but in recessionary phases the ETF’s price may suffer.

From the point of view of credit quality, about 88% of the portfolio is in the upper-tier high yield, while 11% would be the most speculative part, with the potential to enhance overall returns.

Macro: ICE BofA OAS

To analyze the situation of credit spreads, we will use the heatmap in the chart above:

  • Each line corresponds to the credit indices while each column is a month.

  • For the calculation, a z score is applied that indicates how many standard deviations the OAS is above/below its average of the last 21 sessions.

  • The color of each “square” represents the level of spread: warm colors represent greater positive deviation → widening, while cold colors show more compressed spreads than normal.

At the last reading, we have the following situation:

  • CCC & Lower: Last reading is a bluish tint ranging from -0.5 to -1. The previous two months were greenish, around zone 1.

  • B HY OAS: dark blue around the area of -2. The previous two months were in a somewhat dark green zone with value around 0.5 / 1 but closer to 0.5.

  • BB HY OAS: has a slightly lower reading than B, between -1.5 and -2 but closer to -2. The previous two months had a dark bluish green between the 0 and 0.5 zone but closer to zero.

We have drawn on the price graph the general trend that ruled in the different credit tranches being the blue zones moments of dominance in the narrowing and the greens where the widening dominated.

When spreads narrowed, the ETF generated capital gains in addition to its carry, while in periods of widening the movement has been more lateral and even corrective. The last reading in November shows how the spreads have narrowed again and if the trend continues, it is to be expected that the ETF will once again capture capital gains via price while keeping the carry.

In this other article, we analyze inflation by arguing why we thought the Fed was going to lower rates in December, at a time when expectations had turned around. Today, they have returned to a more than likely 87% scenario. Cutting rates improves corporate financing and provides a favorable framework for spreads to remain tight.

To determine whether this narrowing trend can continue, we will review the initial claims data. Despite the upswing between early October and late November, from a macro point of view, the situation is normal and data are within the neutral range when compared to previous recessions:

For us to consider that the situation is becoming alarming, the data would need to overcome the barrier of 350K and with several consecutive weeks showing an accelerating trend in the claims curve.

With the business cycle still stable, together with lower rates improving the financing conditions of the private sector, there is every reason to believe that spreads will remain in a compressed range, which may favor HYG’s capital gains in the medium term and, moreover, allow investors to continue collecting carry.

Conclusion

With a resilient economy, rate cuts and a first indication of a trend of narrowing spreads, conditions are favorable for the trend to continue, so the medium-term horizon could expect capital gains via price plus carry. Our recommendation would be to maintain the position, continue collecting carry and bet on an improvement in the fund’s value.

data.title: ROBO: A Different Vision To Understand Its True Nature
data.description:
It is not the first time I talk about Robo Global® Robotics and Automation Index ETF (ROBO). And in the latest research, I started from a strong assumption, “if I look for competitive alternatives to the tech or AI sector, ROBO is not suitable”. In the end, if compared to this category, it is neither a source of de-correlation (beta), nor of added value in terms of returns (alpha). So the question is: “why have it in the portfolio?”. My answer is that ROBO, in my opinion, is one of the few ETFs truly vertical on the theme of robotics, which must be compared to instruments different from those commonly considered peers. Now I will explain why, but first.

A quick TL;DR

Compared to my latest coverage, here are some key elements to remember in order to understand what we are talking about when discussing ROBO:

  • ROBO is the first ETF in the world dedicated to robotics and automation, and it does so by adopting a physical replication of the ROBO Global Robotics and Automation Index.
  • This passive replication process involves costs that, in my opinion, are very high even for a thematic ETF: there is an Expense Ratio of 0.95% and a bid/ask spread of 0.24%. Even if the premium/discount is practically zero, and there is excellent tracking of the index, a 36% turnover remains. Summarily, this means an annual weight on performance that should not be underestimated.

The strategy of the index

At the head of the “ROBO Global Industry Classification” is the task of selecting the investable universe. Then it applies a filter based on qualitative criteria aimed at assigning a score ranging from 1 to 100. Apparently, only companies with a score above 50 are considered. This is associated with a semi-discretionary process on ROBO, because the committee seems to also have the ability to arbitrarily modify the methodology. And it is a distinctive trait that gives the ETF in question a data-driven touch that I personally appreciate.

Holding Distribution

  • The result is 77 companies, well distributed. Just think that the company with the highest weight inside the index is Symbotic, which accounts for about 2.42%, and the top 10 weighs about 19.7%.
  • This results in a distinctive composition, also at the geographical level: USA 44%, Asia 33%, Europe 23%. Specifically, Japan is overweighted, and already in the top 10 we find 3 holdings to keep in mind, Fanuc, Harmonic Drive, Yaskawa. It is indeed important to remember that 33% of the ETF is in Asia, well above the average of global tech ETFs.
  • And it also has distinctive traits in the market cap mix, composed of Large 51%, Mid 35%, Small 14%. It is one of the very few ETFs that does not have concentration on mega-cap.

Peer

A quick comparison will help to clarify ideas on the matter. In this regard, here is a peer table, which I personally consider quite exhaustive of the ETF market on the robotics theme:

It is impossible not to notice the fact that ROBO is the worst performer among the selected peers. But where is the reason? The reasons, in reality, are different:

  • The cost: among the ETFs considered, it is the one with the highest expenses. This ends up weighing directly on returns, especially when analyzing longer periods.

  • The benchmark: unlike products such as BOTZ, ARKQ, WTAI or THNQ, the other ETFs include a more significant share of companies linked to the artificial intelligence sector, which in recent years has driven much of the growth of the segment. It should be emphasized that ROBO instead is oriented 100% toward the global robotics sector. As a result, it has not benefited from the stock market boost of the hyperscalers post-2022; although it has still been significantly influenced by it. I’ll give you a hint: BOTZ has NVDA in the top 10 for 11%, ARKQ instead has TSLA for 12%, WTAI has the mag7 in the top 10, THNQ is already more diversified, but inside it has names like Alphabet.

Portfolio inclusion

2025 is an exceptional year, +23% YTD, above the ETF’s historical average, a signal that the robotics/automation cycle has entered a period of strong momentum after years of consolidation. The reason? the AI mega trend has also benefited the robotics trend. This is because the increase in CAPEX by AI hyperscalers should sooner or later generate some propulsive effect also outside the inner circle (what I like to call the “OpenAI family”). And the application that immediately comes to mind is robotics: but inside ROBO what is robotics? In the latest coverage, I presented a parallel that in my opinion helps a lot to understand what we are talking about when we talk about robotics in a stock market context: the correct comparison takes place in my opinion with BUYZ (ETF of disruptive commerce) or XLY (ETF of consumer discretionary).

All three show a similar trend, a sign that they are presumably driven by the same drivers, which in this specific case is discretionary or disruptive spending: therefore, in a certain sense, the economic trend.

But it is normal: automation, whether corporate or retail, is actually driven by the same drivers that basically guide XLY or BUYZ.

Expanding the time horizon, it becomes clear how in expansionary cycles, ROBO tends to outperform XLY. So this makes ROBO, in my opinion, a competitive ETF when compared to the right peers. If instead it is evaluated as a substitute for a technological ETF like QQQ or simply for solutions that also include AI inside, naturally a different result emerges.

Risk

It must be said that unlike XLY, which is a diversified ETF on the consumer discretionary theme, ROBO is vertical on the robotics segment. Although it is an expanding sector, it will naturally share the specific risks of the evolution of the robotics segment, and considering that AI has acted as a propulsive driver for 3Y growth, the evolutions of the AI segment will also have an influence on its quotations.

Conclusion

To conclude, I think that ROBO can prove to be a good portfolio ally, as a tactical or satellite component. If compared to other ETFs on the theme of “robotics and AI”, it remains a less competitive solution, but in my opinion it would be a misjudgment to make this comparison. The drivers that stimulate ROBO’s holdings are indeed AI, but also discretionality and disruptiveness in consumption, typical of ETFs like BUYZ and XLY.
